sourceMovie.title: Perfect Blue
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Perfect Blue for the same reason it broke through in 1998: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a thriller rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Satoshi Kon keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The animation and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Paprika
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Perfect Blue clicked for you, Paprika (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and thriller, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Death Note Relight 2: L's Successors
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Perfect Blue clicked for you, Death Note Relight 2: L's Successors (2009) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Perfect Blue?
faq.answer: Try Paprika, Death Note Relight 2: L's Successors, Black Swan—each shares DNA with Perfect Blue while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Perfect Blue?
faq.answer: Perfect Blue (1998) is best described as Animation, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Perfect Blue worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this animation lane.
